---
title: |
  Penalized for Faith: Muslim Bias in Economic Behavioral Games Across 25 EU Countries
subtitle: |
  Evidence from a Conjoint Survey Experiment
abstract: |
  We reanalyize data
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  eval: true
  include: true
draft: true
draft-mode: visible
---

## Introduction {#sec-introduction}

Numerous studies have found a profound Muslim penalty across European societies [@helbling2014opposing; @helbling2012islamophobia].
Yet, less is known about what drives variation in that frequently and robustly observed anti-Muslim bias.
Reanalyzing a large scale conjoint survey experiment fielded in 25 European Union member states [@hahm2023divided; @hahm2024divided], we analyze variation in the experimentally identified Muslim penalty to explore 1) what and how other conjoint features and 2) how individual level covariates affect the estimated Muslim bias.



## Theory {#sec-theory}

## Research Design {#sec-design}

### Data

Our data structure is inherently hierarchical.
Each respondent played three rounds of a Dictator (or Solidarity) game and a Trust game, the latter introduced announced reciprocity in the instructions.
Additionally, given the cross-national structure, respondents are grouped into 25 countries.
In each round, respondents received a fictional co-player with randomized attributes age, gender, social class, nationality, partisan affiliation, EU stance and religion.
Randomization occured in multiple instances: both the order in which attributes were displayed as well as the *feature* of each attribute were both random by design.

To adress the issue of implausible co-player configurations and in light of their research questions, @hahm2024divided implemented four distinct treatment conditions throughout the behavioral games.
In condition one, the conjoint co-player was displayed as a conational, with EU stance and partisan affiliation.
Condition two included conationals with partisan affiliation but no EU stance.
Condition three showed a different but EU nationality and the EU stance attribute, while the final condition four showed only some nationality with no partisan affiliation or EU stance.

Importantly, the religion attribute, consisting the of four features *muslim*, *catholic*, *protestant* and *non-religious* was shown across all of the above mentioned conditions.
This allows us to utilize the full (game) dataset for estimating the Muslim penalty.
One caveat is constituted by the fact that there is not strict control condition for the religion attribute, i.e., in which there was *no* religion feature shown at all.
We refrain from selecting *non-religious* as a substnative control condition as being non-religious itself might not constitute a neutral baseline as it might for instance be perceived differently in eastern European countries with post-communist legacies than elsewhere.
To circumvent the issues with selecting a reference category [see @leeper2020measuring] and to simplify the causal terminology, we collapse the religion feature into a binary treatment indicator $T$ with $T=1$ if the profile was displayed as Muslim and $T=0$ otherwise.[^1]

[^1]: This approach effectively averages the non-Muslim religion feature against the Muslim one. As we are primarily interested in drivers of Muslim bias, not certain facets of Muslim bias itself, we opt for this simpler approach.


### Causal Estimand

Consequently, our outcome $Y_{ric}$ is the amount of tokens allocated in round $r$ by respondent $i$ of country $c$.
Our causal estimand is the average difference in token allocation to Muslim profiles vis-a-vis non-Muslim ones, formally:

$$
\text{ATE} = \mathbb{E}[Y(1) - Y(0)]
$$ {#eq-ate}

Due to randomization by design, this can be estimated as

$$
\text{SATE} = \mathbb{E}[Y | T = 1] - \mathbb{E}[Y | T = 0]
$$ {#eq-sate}

with our interest in treatment effect heterogeneity on covariates $X$ (game and respondent level), we derive the following conditional average treatment effect (CATE):

$$
\text{CATE}(x) =  \mathbb{E}[Y | T = 1, X = x] - \mathbb{E}[Y | T = 0, X = x]
$$ {#eq-cate}


to-do: correct / check full potential outcomes notation derivation, I am right now a little confused about which and how to correctly formalize this

### Estimation Strategy

Given the hierarchical data structure, causal forest [@wager2018estimation; @athey2019estimating] approaches are not optimal, as they assume iid observations and thus potentially return narrower standard errors than appropriate.
We therefore implement Bayesian causal forests (BCT) with randomc intercepts for account for the nested structure in our data. 
@hahn2020bayesian extend Bayesian additive regression trees [@chipman2010bart; @chipman2006bayesian] to BCF by separating modelling of prognostic and treatment effects, thereby reducing regularization-induced confounding [@prevot2025hierarchical]. 
While themselves also requireing independent observations, recent implementations further extended BCF to allow for the inclusion of both random intercepts [@yeager2022synergistic] and random slopes [@yeager2019national], allowing to account for group-level structures in the underlying data.
We adopt this approach for our study to identify treatment heterogeneity in a data-driven way, while accounting for the nested structure using random intercepts and utilizing the full posterior uncertainty quantification for our treatment effect estimates.

We therefore pursue a dual strategy to increase robustness of our heterogeneity analysis.
First, we extend the BCF with random intercepts by @yeager2022synergistic to nested random intercepts, to properly account for our hierarchical data structure with outcomes nested in respondents, which are themselves nested in countries.
Second, we implement a frequentist causal forest [@athey2019generalized] with country fixed effects and respondent level clusters to enhance robustness across estimation procedures.
In both cases, we estimate separate forests for each game.
Consequently, we retreive four main models, BCF and CF for the dictator and trust game.
 

## Empirical Analysis {#sec-analysis}

{{< embed code/01_exploration4presentation.qmd#fig-tokens-to-muslim-profiles >}}

## Conclusion {#sec-conclusion}

\singlespacing

## References

::: {#refs}
:::

## Appendix {.appendix}
